questions.question: Which Vite command creates a new React project?
options: npx create-react-app my-app
npm create vite@latest my-app -- --template react
npm init react my-app
vite create my-app --react
questions.explanation: Create React App is deprecated. The modern standard is `npm create vite@latest my-app -- --template react`. The double `--` separates npm arguments from Vite arguments.

questions.question: Why does JSX use `className` instead of `class`?
options: It is a React-specific convention with no technical reason
HTML5 deprecated the `class` attribute
`class` is a reserved keyword in JavaScript, so the JSX attribute uses `className`
Browsers do not support `class` on custom elements
questions.explanation: JSX compiles to JavaScript. Since `class` is a reserved keyword in JavaScript (used for ES6 class declarations), JSX uses `className` to avoid a syntax conflict.

questions.question:
A component needs to return two sibling `

` elements with no wrapper div. What is the correct approach?
options:
Return them as a JavaScript array: `return [

A

,

B

]`
Wrap them in `<React.Fragment>` or the shorthand `<>...</>`
This is not possible in React -- a wrapper div is required
Use `ReactDOM.renderMultiple()` instead of a component
questions.explanation: React Fragments (`<React.Fragment>` or the shorthand `<>...</>`) let you return multiple sibling elements without adding an extra DOM node. Returning an array also works but requires explicit keys on each element.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly passes a number prop to a component?
options: <Card count="5" />
<Card count=5 />
<Card count={5} />
<Card count=(5) />
questions.explanation: Non-string props must use curly braces: `count={5}`. Using quotes (`count="5"`) passes the string `"5"`, not the number `5`. This matters for arithmetic and strict equality checks.

questions.question: What is wrong with this code: `{items.length && <List items={items} />}`?
options: The `&&` operator cannot be used inside JSX
When `items.length` is `0`, React renders the number `0` in the DOM
This is valid and correct -- there is nothing wrong with it
`items.length` needs to be converted to a string first
questions.explanation: When `items.length` is `0`, the expression short-circuits and returns `0`. React renders `0` (a number) as a text node in the DOM. Fix it with `items.length > 0 && <List />` to ensure the left operand is always a boolean.

questions.question: What is the primary problem with using array index as a `key` prop?
options: Index keys are slower than string keys for React's diffing algorithm
React throws an error if you use index as a key
When the list is reordered or an item is inserted, indices shift -- React incorrectly matches component state to the wrong items
Indexes only work for arrays of primitive values, not objects
questions.explanation: Keys should be stable and based on item identity, not position. When items are reordered or inserted, index-based keys shift -- React then matches state (like input values) to the wrong items, causing hard-to-debug bugs.

questions.question: A component accepts an `items` prop (an array) and needs to add a new item. Which approach is correct?
options: `items.push(newItem)` -- mutate the array directly
`items[items.length] = newItem` -- assign to the next index
Create a new array: `const updated = [...items, newItem]`
Call `items.concat(newItem)` and assign back to `items`
questions.explanation: Props are read-only. Never mutate them. Create a new array using the spread operator: `[...items, newItem]`. This leaves the original array untouched and gives React a new reference it can detect as a change.

questions.question: What is a controlled form input in React?
options: An input wrapped in a `<form>` element with an `onSubmit` handler
An input whose value is stored in React state and updated via `onChange`
An input that uses `ref` to access the DOM value on submit
An input that validates itself using HTML5 constraint validation attributes
questions.explanation: A controlled input binds its `value` prop to React state and updates that state on every `onChange` event. React becomes the single source of truth for the input's value, enabling real-time validation and instant access to the current value.

questions.question: What is the difference between conditional rendering and hiding with CSS `display: none`?
options: There is no functional difference -- both remove the element from the screen
CSS hiding removes the element from the DOM; conditional rendering keeps it mounted
Conditional rendering removes the component from the DOM and destroys its state; CSS hiding keeps it mounted and preserves state
Conditional rendering only works with class components
questions.explanation: Conditional rendering (returning `null`) unmounts the component and destroys all local state -- it will start fresh next time it renders. CSS `display: none` keeps the component mounted in the DOM with state preserved. Choose based on whether you need state preserved between show/hide cycles.

questions.question: What does the `children` prop contain?
options: An array of all child component instances rendered in the app
The JSX placed between a component's opening and closing tags
The DOM nodes of a component's child elements
An array of props passed by the parent component
questions.explanation:
The `children` prop holds whatever JSX is nested between a component's opening and closing tags: `

Hello

`. Inside `Card`, `children` is `

Hello

`. This enables flexible wrapper and layout components that work with any content.
